speaker: interviewer
text: You were just telling me about Warsaw ghetto when you arrived there, and the bridge that connected the two parts of the ghetto. Could you carry on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, the first place we lived in was [? Sliska. ?] Street was called [? Sliska. ?] And as I said before, because we were refugees, we rented a room with a family. And well, I don't remember very much. I remember that things were very tough and things. But not as tough as, as they later on became. And this family was very nice to us. And we had just one room. And I had friends. And I remember that I was playing with my friends. I remember I was putting on my mother's clothes and doing other things I suppose that little girls do, you know. And we were playing grown ups, and it was dolls and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was the very first time in my life that we, I ate cholent. Do you know what cholent is? Because there was a shortage of food, we did-- did it together with the people that rented us a room.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were, became very friendly with my parents because I think they were more or less the same age group. And I remember that we, together with them, we prepared potatoes, and we prepared the cabbage in the cellar.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we used to-- there was no other fat available, but what do they call it? Bacon, you know? So we used-- my mother had-- was you know, had her [INAUDIBLE] of, of lard. And she, because there was no refrigerator, we put it on the boxes outside the apartment because it was cold.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that stage, just-- no, I didn't learn at that stage yet. But I had a lot of friends. There were the girls in the, in the house where I lived. And I played a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you arrived in the ghetto, do you remember roughly what time it was? What season it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that I arrived in ghetto springtime, I would say. Maybe. I don't know exactly. I really don't remember exactly. But I, I know that it-- the winter was over. Winter was over. And I have very dim memories of the beginning of the ghetto. I can't really tell you very much. Later on, maybe because I got older, or things were happening in a more, you know, tragic way, that I remember more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when you started school again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I think I really started in the [? Sliska. ?] Yes, I did. Now, now that I, you know, recall, I did. It was like that. Let me explain to you. The school that I went to in, in Lodz, six families came from Lodz to Warsaw. And there were six girls from my class. And the schoolteacher who was-- well, she was a wonderful lady-- who was our schoolteacher from beginning, came also. They also ran away from Lodz. These were all people, refugees, who were running away from the Germans because of the Third Reich. Because things in Lodz started to get bad straight away. And this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the teacher's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. My word. She had a terrific influence in my life because she was a very wonderful person. Her name was Vera Szildkret. And she had no means of survival either. She had no means to support herself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she must have come to the parents, which I don't know, and must have said, look, I am here. And there are six girls from the class. And you're not allowed really to learn in Warsaw ghetto. You were not allowed to have lessons. It was quite illegal what we did. And because of-- it was illegal, we did it every day in somebody's else's place so we won't get caught.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she used to teach us, the six girls. And that friend of mine, Hanka, she was there too. And we-- we used to walk every day to a different place. And because there were only six of us, and she was such a wonderful teacher, I think that we must have been doing two, three grades in one year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell her name for the interview, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I, I don't know whether I can. Yeah well, it'll be very hard. It's a long name. S-Z-I-L-D-K-R-E-T, I think. Szildkret, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember particular lessons that she taught you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I, I remember I was very good-- I was not, never very good for maths. I didn't like it very much. But I remember learning a lot about geography and writing a lot of essays on geography. And somehow I always used to come first. And she always used to admire my, my essays on geography. And I remember when we used to go to, to the school-- oh well, it wasn't school-- to the, to the lessons, we always used to play afterwards. We used to play. There was a--it was a ball in the courtyard. And it was actually-- although it was such a tragic time for all the Jewish people-- it was a very happy time for me. Because I was with girls, and I was walking every day. And we-- my girlfriend Hanka, she used to read a lot about Sherlock Holmes. [LAUGHS] And on the way, she was-- always used to tell me all the stories about Sherlock Holmes. I used to tell her the story that I read. And we played afterwards. And we didn't stay very long in [? Sliska ?] because the Germans liquidated the small ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Sliska ?] was the small ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I don't remember how long we stayed there. But they liquidated the small ghetto and put all the Jewish people from the small ghetto together with the others in the large ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because they wanted-- for, for their plans that they had for us, now that I look back, I can see they wanted everybody congregated so it'd be easier for them to get rid of us. You see? Did your lessons continue in the big ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: My lessons continued, yes. And it was a very wonderful part of, of, of, of, of this Warsaw ghetto experience. And also at that time, when we shifted to the big ghetto, we rented a room from a childless couple. And the street name was Rgrodewa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] R-G-R-O-D-E-W-A, Rgrodewa, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] 29. I think. Yes. And we, we rented the room for the end. That's where we lived in, in, in the beginning in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: You lived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived there, yes. My parents rented a room. And it was horrible, because there was a terrible lot of bedbugs, I remember. And they used to, you know, bite my, my, my brother and myself. You know, we used to be bitten all over. And it was a beginning of terrible things already. Because when I was going to the lessons, there was a terrible hunger in Warsaw already. And there was all the time, there were Germans you know, on motorbikes and cars going through the streets. And every time I saw the Germans, I, I was very, very frightened. And also when I was going to the lessons, I could see the children and with their swollen tummies laying on the street. And there was a lot of dead children laying there too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my mother used to give me breads with something to take to the lessons. But you know what they did in ghetto when they saw that you were wearing-- that you were carrying a parcel of food? They used to run to you and snatch it and run away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I always saw to it that somebody snatched my food. I made it obvious that I am carrying food because I knew how hungry those children were, and I wanted them to snatch my food. And that was already very bad times. In the beginning, things were not too bad. In the-- you wouldn't believe in the beginning, the Germans allow coffee lunches and they had-- but that was only very short time. And we had concerts. My father used to take me in the morning to symphony concerts every few weeks. They took a flat upstairs, two flats, and they made a very big one out of it, and they had concerts there. My father used to take me. My father used to take me everywhere, you know. And going back to Lodz, he used to take me to pictures. I saw Shirley Temple, all Shirley Temple films before the war. And anyway, to make a long story short, that was the beginning, you know. But I think that things were-- I was all the time scared. We're all scared. There was such a fear because we knew that they are-- there was no security and no safety. And we knew that they were coming to houses and beating people up, beating people up in the street and taking people off the street and putting them. We saw the lorries passing with, with people that were grabbed from the street and put on the lorries, you know. So you never, ever felt very safe. And also, I forgot to tell you, in Lodz, we used to wear an yellow Star of David. It had to be put on your clothes. But in Warsaw, you had to wear an armband. A white armband with a blue Star of David.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to carry any special documents with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I, I don't remember carrying any documents. But people did, yes. You had to have papers with you. But maybe because I was a child, I don't know. But I remember this white armband and the yellow one in, in, in Lodz. You see, I'm not going to tell you things chronically. I'm terribly sorry about that. As my memory takes me, that's where I'm going to tell, tell it to you. Going back to Lodz, I remember that we're not allowed after to go to-- already in certain streets we're not allowed to move, even though there was no ghetto. Like the street we lived in, [INAUDIBLE], we're not allowed to, allowed anymore to walk in that street. But if you had the yellow star, they could get you straight away. So my mother used to put a, a raincoat, a rain cape over my clothes so they never saw it. And that's the way I came home. And going back to Warsaw, we're already in the ghetto so there was no problem with that. We're allowed to move in the ghetto up to a certain time. I think-- I don't remember what time. But I think about 7:00, 8 o'clock or 6 o'clock in the evening. After that, we were not allowed to move out of the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who decided where the lessons were going to be?

speaker: interviewee
text: The teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: She told you every night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep. Every day. It was during the day. Every day she told us, when we finished, she said tomorrow we're having in such and such a place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going back to where you lived, do you remember your room? Where you lived with your parents and your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was a sort of a biggish room. And my parents had a double bed and my brother and I slept on, on those makeshift beds, you know, that you roll up during the night-- during the day. And we had the use of the kitchen. These people were not very happy that we lived there because these people had no children, and they were elderly. And I don't think they liked, liked to have us there. But they had to have refugees. They had to rent room to somebody. That was by the Germans' orders. And I, I, I remember also these people, they hadn't suffered as much as we did. Because we already suffered in Lodz a lot, you know. And they couldn't understand. They didn't know what's going to happen to them later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what your father did during the day? How he supported you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember. He-- I think he did something because he was not at home. But I don't think I, I remember what he did, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what your mother did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My mother was-- you know, I suppose my father was there too. He was staying in the queues for food. Everything was rationed. We had coupons. And although you know the Joint, the American Joint, used to send us-- for Pesach for instance, they used to send us a lot of very wonderful foods to Warsaw ghetto. But we never received it. The Germans took the food. And what we got was, everything was ersatz. I don't know whether you know what ersatz means. Was artificial. Like the [INAUDIBLE] was artificial, everything was artificial. My mother was always queuing up and cooking, you know. I remember [LAUGHS] we made spinach out of you know, the leaves of the beetroots. You don't-- you have no idea what a wonderful spinach you can make. It tastes exactly like spinach. And also we were very lucky. We couldn't get any other meat except horse meat, and that not very often. And we started buying horse meat and eating horse meat. And my father always used to joke, he used to walk in the middle of street pretending he is a horse. Anyway, this is all childish, because things were starting to get grimmer and grimmer. And in-- I remember that I had a, a lot of friends there. And in that Rgrodewa 29, we had an orphanage. And the orphanage was created right during the war. And I think it was created already for children who were orphaned during the war. Their parents were killed. And this orphanage was, took a whole floor in this big house that we lived, you know. And the people who live in the house, they looked after the orphanage, you know. They used to play cards and take money. They used to make all different kind of evenings and make money to support this orphanage. And I remember that we children, we used to do the same. We used to make pantomime and sell tickets for you know, for very little money and bring money to this orphanage. And they started to grab people like-- I remember when things started to get very grim. I remember that I heard-- we heard in-- in Warsaw that the president of the Warsaw Committee-- Adam Czerniakow was his name-- committed suicide. What happened was apparently the Germans went up to him and wanted him to sign a document to say that he allows-- which is ridiculous-- that he allows the Germans to take the Jews wherever they want to take them. And the Germans didn't need any permission to do that because they did it anyhow. But I think that they wanted it for later on probably, to shift the responsibility or something like that. And I think that this committee, which it was called the-- like a Jewish board of deputies. You know, everything, all the official things went through the Jewish board of deputies. If there was an order from the Germans, it went through the Jewish board of deputies and they told the population what to do. This-- when they went to him, he-- that Adam Czerniakow-- he was a lawyer. He knew probably where they were going to take the Jewish people. And he didn't want to sign.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they gave him 24 hours to sign. And when they came the next day to-- for the signature, he was gone. He committed suicide. Did you know about--

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't want to sign.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know about that as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I knew about that from my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: He told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He told us, yes. He told-- I, I remember the, the, the grownups. See, because the people used-- never used to go out at night, people were very friendly and very sociable towards each other. And they used to meet each, each other's places at, in the evening. Oh, my parents had a lot of friends. And, and that's how it started. I think that's how he started to send the Jewish people to Treblinka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we go back a little bit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me again about the pantomimes and the tickets you used to sell for the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, [LAUGHS] that was really-- I used to be-- take part in pantomimes in, in, in school when I was a little girl. I was not much bigger then. But you know, I, I had the ability to perform very nicely on the stage. And the teachers always picked me for the, for the main part. And because-- maybe because I wasn't shy as a child. I was never shy. And when we made little pantomimes you know, we made all kinds of different paper costumes. And we were performing and charging the other kids from the house, you know, who lived there, a little, you know, money to come in and, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember which parts you played in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't remember the pantomimes anymore now. But, but we had a very nice time doing it, apart from those just horrible clouds that were coming--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and gathering.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go to the orphanage and see the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. Yes, I went to the orphanage. I-- it was a very lovely orphanage. It was very clean, and it was-- the children were well fed and well looked after. And they were dressed all in the same uniform. And perhaps I better tell you something about what happened to the children's orphanage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the people who ran the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the people who ran the orphanage, but except for one man who was a patron of the orphanage. And I-- I don't remember seeing him till much later, and his name was Janusz Korczak-- Dr. Korczak. I don't know whether you ever heard of him. Dr. Korczak was a, a man who has written a lot of children stories. And I remember reading them all. And in his, in his books, he tried to point out to people that really there is not much different between Jewish children and non-Jewish children. They're all human beings and all children. And you know, he always tried to reconcile the Poles with the Jews, not to-- he tried to, to make the hatred go away. But of course, he didn't succeed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever meet him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't meet him personally, but I saw him going to his death, which I was just about to tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me. Sorry. Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Would you like to know? Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Please tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one day the Germans came, and they closed up the whole house and they took everybody from the, from the apartments onto the courtyard. And we were put-- I don't remember how we were put, but we were standing all there. And then, you see, the Germans first, when they were cleaning up the ghetto-- when I was cleaning up the ghetto is a terrible expression, but that's exactly what they were doing-- they, they, they first, they took people off the street that were homeless and, and, and people who were very poor and you know, beggars who, probably before the war, were very cultured people. But during, due to the circumstances, they were reduced to being beggars. And they straight away took them away. They must have killed them straight away. And then they started with orphanages. And that orphanage, when they took us down in the courtyard, they put those children in pairs. And that day, they took out a lot of people from the courtyard that were there. And they took the orphanage, the children were marching, put in pairs. And Korczak, Dr. Korczak was standing next to the first pair. And on the other side was standing a, a woman. I think that the woman was a, a teacher or, or, or a person who was employed there to look after the children. And later on, I found out-- I just saw them marching out. And I saw Korczak picking up a child and putting him like that and marching out of, of the, of the courtyard. And I didn't know it at the time, but later on I found out that he was allowed to stay. Apparently, the SS, the Germans, said to him, you don't have to go with the orphanage. But that's when he picked up the child and, and marched out very proudly with his head held high with the children. And as I said, there was a lot of people who were taken that day. Now I'm going to tell you something. Our next door neighbors was a young couple with a little girl. It's relevant, otherwise I wouldn't bring it up. And there was an elderly couple that were the parents. Like the parents, a daughter with her husband, and a little girl. And that day, the Germans took away the mother, the daughter, and the granddaughter. The men were not at home. And the men-- they were very, very friendly with my parents, the whole family. And the men apparently, were very wealthy people who had timber yards. And they had-- they were-- had a lot of German friends and German-- before the war-- German customers. And when they took away the, the wives and the child, when they came home and saw it, they became absolutely frantic. And they got in touch with the German clients. I know it all from my parents. I didn't know it at the time. And they got hold of the German clients, and they offered an enormous amount of money. First of all, they said follow, follow what happened to, to, to, to the wives, you know. Because they were put on a train. And the car followed the train. With the Germans followed the, the train. And they were, you know, authorized to offer an enormous amount of money to get those women back, you know. Like the, you know, the mother and the daughter and the granddaughter. The-- I think the granddaughter was, at the time, a little girl, maybe three or four years old. The name of the, of the parents was Francusz. And the name of the, of the married couple, young married couple, was Lerner. And the Germans came back. And that's how we found out where they're taking the Jewish people. Because the Germans came back and they told this to men, that there is no money in the world that can buy back those women because the train went to Treblinka, and everybody that was unloaded from the train was killed. And that's my father knew we were the first people in the Warsaw ghetto to know-- one of the first people-- to know what the Jew, doing with the Jewish people. And therefore, my father had developed a, a policy. And he said to us, as little as we were, but I understood and that saved my life many times. He said, remember one thing. Whatever the Germans tell you to do, you do the opposite. Because if you do what they tell you to do, you're going to die.

speaker: interviewer
text: He used those words?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They're going to kill us. You're going to die. And then they were making the ghetto smaller and smaller because they were forever sending people away. And we started working for a-- they only now kept people who were working for Germans in factories and producing something for Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your little brother do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your little brother do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we smuggled him all the time. We smuggled him to work. [LAUGHS] We worked in a factory. In a socks factory. It was called Schultz. Small Schultz, because there was a large Schultz who was making different things. And by that time, we didn't live anymore in Rgrodewa. We live, lived in [? Lashna ?] 76. And funnily enough, the factory-- it's really a very funny coincidence. They made the factory Rgrodewa 29 where we lived before. And my father was very friendly with the owner of the house. And because we were going every day there to work, my father and the owner of the house and another man, an engineer, they made a bunker for us in the cellar. And the caretaker was Polish. He wasn't Jewish. And the bunker was prepared with everything. There was water and-- and food and clothes. And only the caretaker knew about it. And we knew that one day they're going to come and get us. So we decided to stay the nights over in there and never come back to the ghetto. But I'm running ahead of myself. One day they made what they called-- well, as I said, I was working there. My parents were working there. What they used to do, the Germans, they used to bring socks from the front. And what they-- we did in the factory, we used to cut off used socks from the soldiers. We used to cut off the foot pad and used to make new-- we only kept the uppers-- and we used to make the new foot pad. And [LAUGHS] I had this wonder-- I was so fast I used to put it on combs. I don't know whether you would realize what it is. You know, when it was cut off, it had little eyes. And I used to put the little eyes on combs and give it to the machinist. And the machinist used to put it on the machine and knit. And I was so fast, I used to work at night shift. I was so fast that I used to get extra bread, you know. And that way we survived, because we were at work. And we used to smuggle my brother. Every day we went to work, we used to smuggle my, my brother with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you smuggle him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, because we used to work in a group. We used to walk in a group. So he was very little. We-- that's the way we smuggled him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what food were you eating at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: What food were you eating at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: What food? Not much of a food. Mostly breads, you know. But we were very lucky to have bread. As I said to you, people were-- the, the things that I have seen in Warsaw ghetto, in the street, will remain in my mind forever. The swollen children. You have never seen swollen children like that. This is, this is-- I have never seen it again. Their bellies and their, and their faces and everything was swollen. And they were dying in front of everybody's eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were very lucky to have food to eat, you know. And I'm going to tell you what happened at the [? Mila ?] 18. Thank you.
